seoTitle: Slkor Power MOSFETs, IGBTs, SiC Devices | Product Selection Guide | LiTong Distributor
seoDescription: Explore Slkor's comprehensive range of power semiconductors including MOSFETs, IGBTs, SiC devices, and power management ICs. Find the right device for your application with our selection guide.

faqs.question: What types of MOSFETs does Slkor offer?
faqs.answer: Slkor offers a comprehensive range of MOSFETs including: Low-voltage MOSFETs (20V-100V) for battery management and load switching with ultra-low RDS(on); High-voltage MOSFETs (500V-900V) for power supplies and lighting applications; Super-junction MOSFETs for high-efficiency switching power supplies; and Automotive-grade MOSFETs qualified to AEC-Q101. The product line covers current ratings from milliamps to over 100A, with various package options including SOT-23, SOP-8, TO-220, TO-247, and DPAK.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our MOSFET selection guide to find the optimal device based on voltage, current, and package requirements. Contact our FAE team for application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right IGBT for my motor drive application?
faqs.answer: Selecting an IGBT for motor drives requires considering several key parameters: Voltage rating should be at least 1.5x the DC bus voltage (typically 600V or 1200V); Current rating based on motor power and overload requirements; Switching frequency - standard IGBTs for <20kHz, fast IGBTs for 20-50kHz; and Package type based on power dissipation and thermal management. Slkor's IGBT portfolio includes 600V-1200V devices with current ratings from 10A to 100A, suitable for motor drives from fractional horsepower to tens of kilowatts.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide your motor specifications (voltage, power, switching frequency) and our FAE team will recommend the optimal IGBT with thermal calculations and driver circuit design support.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of Slkor SiC devices over silicon MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Slkor's SiC (Silicon Carbide) devices offer significant advantages over traditional silicon MOSFETs: Higher breakdown voltage with lower on-resistance, enabling reduced conduction losses; Faster switching speeds (3-5x faster) allowing higher frequency operation and smaller passive components; Better thermal conductivity enabling higher operating temperatures and improved heat dissipation; Lower switching losses reducing EMI and improving system efficiency by 2-5%; and Smaller die size for equivalent current capability. These benefits make SiC ideal for EV chargers, solar inverters, and high-density power supplies.
faqs.decisionGuide: For applications requiring highest efficiency and power density, evaluate Slkor's SiC MOSFETs and diodes. Request evaluation boards and application notes from our technical team.

faqs.question: Can Slkor MOSFETs replace international brand parts?
faqs.answer: Yes, many Slkor MOSFETs can serve as drop-in replacements for international brand parts with comparable or better electrical parameters. Slkor offers cross-reference guides mapping their devices to popular parts from Infineon, ON Semiconductor, Vishay, and others. Key considerations for replacement include: Voltage rating must meet or exceed original; RDS(on) should be equal or lower; Gate charge and switching characteristics should be compatible; and Package must be pin-compatible. Slkor's technical team can provide detailed comparison data and replacement recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide the part number you need to replace and our FAE team will identify the best Slkor alternative with full parameter comparison and sample support.

faqs.question: What packages are available for Slkor power devices?
faqs.answer: Slkor power devices are available in a wide range of packages to suit different applications: Small signal packages (SOT-23, SOT-89) for low-power switching; Surface mount power packages (SOP-8, DFN, PowerPAK) for medium power with automated assembly; Through-hole packages (TO-220, TO-247, TO-263/DPAK) for high-power applications requiring good heat sinking; and Isolated packages for high-voltage applications. The package selection depends on power dissipation, thermal requirements, assembly process, and board space constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider thermal requirements and assembly process when selecting packages. Our FAE team can provide thermal modeling and PCB layout recommendations for optimal heat dissipation.

categories.name: MOSFETs
categories.description: Slkor's MOSFET portfolio includes low-voltage, high-voltage, and super-junction power MOSFETs designed for switching applications in consumer electronics, industrial equipment, and power supplies. These devices feature low on-resistance, fast switching speeds, and robust avalanche capability. The product line covers voltage ratings from 20V to 900V and current ratings up to 100A, available in various packages from small SOT-23 to large TO-247.
categories.longDescription: Slkor's MOSFET portfolio, available through LiTong distributor, covers the full spectrum of power switching applications with comprehensive selection guide support. Low-voltage MOSFETs (20V-100V) with ultra-low RDS(on) are ideal for battery management, load switching, and synchronous rectification in DC-DC converters. High-voltage MOSFETs (500V-900V) serve AC-DC power supplies, LED lighting, and appliance applications. Super-junction technology enables breakthrough performance in high-voltage applications with significantly lower RDS(on) than conventional planar MOSFETs. All devices are manufactured using advanced trench gate technology for optimal switching characteristics and reliability.

series.name: SL Series Low-Voltage
series.description: 20V-100V MOSFETs with ultra-low RDS(on) for battery and load switch applications

series.name: SL Series High-Voltage
series.description: 500V-900V MOSFETs for power supplies and lighting applications

selectionGuide.title: MOSFET Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right MOSFET for your application

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right Slkor MOSFET?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting Slkor MOSFETs based on voltage, current, and application requirements

faqs.question: What is the difference between low-voltage and high-voltage MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Low-voltage MOSFETs (20V-100V) are optimized for battery-powered and low-voltage DC applications with extremely low RDS(on) to minimize conduction losses. They typically have lower gate charge for faster switching. High-voltage MOSFETs (500V-900V) are designed for AC-DC power supplies and must withstand high voltage stress. They have higher RDS(on) but are optimized for switching losses and EMI performance. The choice depends on your application's voltage level and whether the priority is conduction loss (low-voltage) or blocking voltage capability (high-voltage).
faqs.decisionGuide: For battery applications (3.3V-48V), use low-voltage MOSFETs. For AC-DC supplies from mains voltage, use 600V-900V high-voltage MOSFETs. Contact our FAE team for specific recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I calculate conduction losses in a MOSFET?
faqs.answer: Conduction loss in a MOSFET is calculated using P = I² × RDS(on) × D, where I is the RMS current through the device, RDS(on) is the on-resistance at operating temperature, and D is the duty cycle. Note that RDS(on) increases with temperature - typically 1.5x to 2x higher at 100°C than at 25°C. For accurate calculations, use the maximum RDS(on) from the datasheet at your expected operating temperature. Also consider that RDS(on) increases with gate voltage - ensure your drive voltage provides the specified RDS(on).
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our online MOSFET loss calculator or contact our FAE team for detailed thermal and loss analysis for your specific application conditions.

faqs.question: What is the significance of gate charge (Qg) in MOSFET selection?
faqs.answer: Gate charge (Qg) determines the switching speed and drive power requirements of a MOSFET. Lower Qg enables faster switching (reducing switching losses) and requires less drive current from the gate driver. Total gate charge consists of Qgs (gate-source) and Qgd (gate-drain, also called Miller charge). The Miller charge is particularly important as it determines the voltage fall time during switching. For high-frequency applications (>100kHz), select MOSFETs with low Qg and Qgd to minimize switching losses and EMI.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high-frequency switching applications, prioritize low Qg MOSFETs. Our FAE team can recommend optimal devices based on your switching frequency and driver capability.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect MOSFET performance?
faqs.answer: Temperature significantly affects MOSFET performance in several ways: RDS(on) increases approximately 0.4% per °C, meaning a device with 10mΩ at 25°C may have 15-20mΩ at 100°C; Maximum current rating decreases at higher temperatures due to increased RDS(on) and reduced thermal margin; Gate threshold voltage decreases with temperature, potentially affecting turn-off behavior; and Leakage current increases exponentially with temperature. Always design for worst-case operating temperatures and use the RDS(on) values specified at elevated temperatures in the datasheet.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design thermal management for maximum ambient temperature plus self-heating. Our FAE team can provide thermal modeling and heatsink selection guidance.

faqs.question: What is avalanche rating in MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Avalanche rating indicates a MOSFET's ability to withstand voltage transients that exceed its breakdown voltage. When the drain voltage exceeds V(BR)DSS, the MOSFET enters avalanche breakdown, conducting current while dissipating energy. The avalanche energy rating (EAS) specifies how much energy the device can safely absorb in a single pulse. This is important for inductive load switching where voltage spikes occur. Slkor MOSFETs are avalanche rated, providing robustness against unexpected voltage transients without external protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: For inductive loads (motors, solenoids, relays), select avalanche-rated MOSFETs or add external protection. Contact our FAE team for inductive switching application guidance.

products.name: 30V N-Channel MOSFET
products.shortDescription: Low-voltage power MOSFET with 4.1A continuous drain current and 6mΩ typical RDS(on) in compact SOT-23 package
descriptionParagraphs: SL3407 is a 30V N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET designed for low-voltage switching applications including battery management, load switching, and DC-DC converters.

With typical RDS(on) of only 6mΩ at 4.5V gate drive, this device minimizes conduction losses in battery-powered applications. The SOT-23 package provides a compact footprint for space-constrained designs.
The device features low gate charge for fast switching, logic-level gate drive compatibility, and excellent thermal performance for its package size.
features: Ultra-low RDS(on) of 6mΩ minimizes conduction losses
Fast switching with low gate charge
Compact SOT-23 package for space-constrained designs
Avalanche energy rated for robust operation

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Electronics
faeReview.content: SL3407 is my go-to recommendation for low-voltage battery switching applications. The 6mΩ RDS(on) at logic-level drive is impressive for a SOT-23 device, making it ideal for single-cell Li-ion applications where every milliohm counts. I've successfully used this part in numerous wearable and IoT designs where space and efficiency are critical. The logic-level threshold ensures reliable switching even at low battery voltages. For best performance, I recommend using a 4.7Ω gate resistor to control switching speed and minimize EMI. The device has proven very reliable in high-volume production.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low 6mΩ RDS(on) in compact SOT-23 package

alternativeParts.comparison: SL3407=><SI2302: Output current 4.1A > 2.9A (+41%), Package SOT-23 = SOT-23 (same), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Lower RDS(on) and higher current rating provide better performance
alternativeParts.useCase: Upgrade for lower conduction loss in existing designs

alternativeParts.comparison: SL3407=><AO3407: Output current 4.1A > 3.8A (+8%), Package SOT-23 = SOT-23 (same), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Lower RDS(on) with similar voltage and current ratings

companionParts.description: Complementary P-channel MOSFET for H-bridge designs
Low-side gate driver for MOSFET control
LDO regulator for gate drive power supply

faqs.question: What is the minimum gate voltage required to fully turn on SL3407?
faqs.answer: SL3407 can be fully turned on with a gate voltage of 4.5V, achieving the specified 6mΩ RDS(on). At 2.5V gate drive, the device will conduct but with higher RDS(on). For logic-level applications using 3.3V or 2.5V logic, ensure your driver can provide sufficient voltage. The threshold voltage is 1.0V-2.5V, but operation near threshold results in high RDS(on) and excessive heating. I recommend minimum 3.0V gate drive for acceptable performance, with 4.5V or higher for optimal low RDS(on).
faqs.decisionGuide: For 3.3V logic applications, verify your driver output voltage under load. Use a gate driver IC if the logic output cannot provide adequate drive. Contact our FAE team for driver selection guidance.

faqs.question: How much power can SL3407 dissipate in SOT-23 package?
faqs.answer: The maximum power dissipation depends on ambient temperature and thermal management. With standard PCB copper (minimum pad), thermal resistance RthJA is approximately 200°C/W. At 25°C ambient, maximum power is about 0.6W (150°C junction limit). With enhanced thermal design (larger copper pours, thermal vias), RthJA can improve to 100°C/W, allowing 1.25W dissipation. For continuous operation, I recommend keeping junction temperature below 100°C for long-term reliability. Calculate actual power as P = I² × RDS(on), using the elevated temperature RDS(on) value.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high-current applications, use large copper pours and multiple thermal vias. Consider using multiple parallel devices if power dissipation exceeds package capability. Our FAE team can provide thermal modeling assistance.

faqs.question: Can SL3407 be used for hot-swap applications?
faqs.answer: While SL3407 can be used for hot-swap, it requires careful design. The device lacks integrated current limiting and soft-start features found in dedicated hot-swap controllers. For simple hot-swap, use a slow-turn-on circuit with an RC network on the gate to limit inrush current. The 4.1A current rating provides margin for capacitor charging, but ensure your load capacitance doesn't exceed the MOSFET's SOA during startup. For robust hot-swap protection, I recommend adding a dedicated hot-swap controller IC with current sensing and foldback protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: For simple low-cost hot-swap, SL3407 with proper gate control can work. For systems requiring protection and monitoring, use a dedicated hot-swap controller. Contact our FAE team for hot-swap circuit design guidance.

faqs.question: What is the maximum switching frequency for SL3407?
faqs.answer: SL3407 can switch at frequencies up to several MHz, but practical limits depend on gate drive capability and switching losses. With 12nC total gate charge, driving at 1MHz requires 12mA average gate current. The switching times (8ns rise, 15ns fall) support high-frequency operation. However, switching losses increase linearly with frequency. For hard-switching applications, I recommend keeping frequencies below 500kHz to maintain reasonable efficiency. For synchronous rectification in DC-DC converters, 1-2MHz is achievable with proper gate drive and minimal dead time.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high-frequency applications, use a dedicated gate driver IC with adequate drive current. Minimize gate loop inductance with short, wide traces. Our FAE team can review your switching circuit design.

faqs.question: How does SL3407 compare to using a load switch IC?
faqs.answer: Compared to integrated load switch ICs, SL3407 offers lower RDS(on) at a lower cost but requires external protection circuitry. Load switch ICs typically include features like slew rate control, current limiting, and thermal protection in one package. SL3407 provides better conduction efficiency (6mΩ vs 20-50mΩ for load switches) and higher current capability. The choice depends on your priorities: use SL3407 for lowest cost and best efficiency in simple applications; use load switch ICs when integrated protection features are required and slightly higher RDS(on) is acceptable.
faqs.decisionGuide: For cost-sensitive designs prioritizing efficiency, SL3407 is the better choice. For designs requiring protection features without external circuitry, consider integrated load switches. Our FAE team can help evaluate trade-offs for your specific application.

categories.name: IGBTs
categories.description: Slkor's IGBT portfolio includes discrete IGBTs for motor drives, inverters, and power conversion applications. These devices combine the high input impedance of MOSFETs with the low conduction losses of bipolar transistors, making them ideal for high-voltage, high-current switching applications.
categories.longDescription: Slkor IGBTs, available through LiTong distributor, are designed for high-efficiency power conversion in industrial and automotive applications with comprehensive selection guide support. The product line includes 600V and 1200V devices with current ratings from 10A to 100A, suitable for motor drives from fractional horsepower to tens of kilowatts. These IGBTs feature low saturation voltage, fast switching, and excellent short-circuit withstand capability. Applications include variable frequency drives, servo systems, EV motor controllers, solar inverters, and induction heating equipment.

series.name: SL T Series
series.description: Standard IGBTs for general-purpose motor drives and inverters

selectionGuide.title: IGBT Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right IGBT for your motor drive or inverter application

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right Slkor IGBT?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting Slkor IGBTs for motor drives and power conversion

faqs.question: When should I choose an IGBT over a MOSFET?
faqs.answer: Choose IGBTs over MOSFETs when: Operating voltage is above 300-400V DC (IGBTs have lower conduction losses at high voltage); Current is above 10-20A (IGBTs are more cost-effective at high currents); Switching frequency is below 50kHz (IGBTs have higher switching losses than MOSFETs); and Cost is a major factor (IGBTs are typically cheaper than high-voltage MOSFETs for equivalent current). MOSFETs are better for high-frequency applications (>100kHz) and lower voltage (<200V) where their lower switching losses and simpler drive requirements provide advantages.
faqs.decisionGuide: For motor drives above 1kW and AC input supplies, IGBTs are usually the better choice. Contact our FAE team for detailed comparison based on your specific voltage, current, and frequency requirements.

faqs.question: What is the difference between field-stop and punch-through IGBT technology?
faqs.answer: Field-stop (FS) technology is an advancement over traditional punch-through (PT) and non-punch-through (NPT) IGBT structures. FS IGBTs use a lightly doped n-drift region with a field-stop layer near the collector, combining the low conduction losses of PT IGBTs with the ruggedness of NPT IGBTs. Benefits include: Lower VCE(sat) for reduced conduction losses; Faster switching with lower switching losses; Better safe operating area (SOA) for short-circuit withstand; and Lower tail current for reduced turn-off losses. Slkor's IGBT portfolio uses field-stop technology for optimal performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: For new designs, always select field-stop IGBTs for best efficiency and ruggedness. Contact our FAE team for detailed technology comparison and selection guidance.

faqs.question: How do I calculate IGBT losses for thermal design?
faqs.answer: IGBT total losses consist of conduction losses and switching losses. Conduction loss is Pcond = VCE(sat) × IC × D, where D is duty cycle. Use VCE(sat) at operating temperature (typically 1.5x the 25°C value). Switching loss is Psw = (Eon + Eoff) × fsw, where Eon and Eoff are switching energies from the datasheet at your operating current and voltage. For inverter applications, calculate losses at multiple operating points and use the weighted average. Don't forget to include diode losses if using the integrated anti-parallel diode.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our online loss calculator for accurate thermal design. Contact our FAE team for thermal modeling and heatsink recommendations.

faqs.question: What gate drive voltage is recommended for IGBTs?
faqs.answer: IGBTs should be driven with +15V for turn-on to achieve the specified low VCE(sat). For turn-off, negative gate voltage of -5V to -8V is strongly recommended. The negative voltage provides several benefits: faster turn-off reducing switching losses; improved noise immunity preventing false turn-on from dv/dt; better short-circuit protection response; and more robust operation in noisy industrial environments. A typical gate drive scheme uses +15V/-8V. If unipolar drive must be used (0V/+15V), ensure the gate-emitter path has very low impedance and consider adding a Miller clamp circuit.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use a gate driver IC with built-in negative voltage capability or a bootstrap circuit with negative bias. Contact our FAE team for gate driver recommendations and circuit design.

faqs.question: How do I prevent shoot-through in IGBT bridge circuits?
faqs.answer: Shoot-through occurs when both high-side and low-side IGBTs conduct simultaneously, causing a short circuit across the DC bus. Prevention methods include: Adequate dead time (typically 1-3μs) inserted between turning off one device and turning on the other; Using gate drivers with built-in dead time generation and interlock protection; Implementing hardware interlocks that prevent simultaneous conduction; Adding current sensing with fast overcurrent shutdown (<2μs); and Using desaturation detection to detect shoot-through and initiate soft shutdown. The dead time must account for IGBT turn-off delay variations with temperature and manufacturing tolerance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 2μs dead time and verify with oscilloscope measurements. Contact our FAE team for dead time optimization and protection circuit design.

products.name: 650V 20A IGBT
products.shortDescription: Field-stop IGBT with 20A collector current and 1.6V typical VCE(sat) for motor drive applications
descriptionParagraphs: SL20T65F is a 650V field-stop trench IGBT designed for high-efficiency motor drives and power conversion applications.
With 20A continuous collector current and low saturation voltage of 1.6V typical, this IGBT delivers excellent efficiency in hard-switching applications.
The device includes a fast-recovery anti-parallel diode for freewheeling operation, making it ideal for bridge configurations in motor drives.
features: Field-stop technology for low conduction and switching losses
Low VCE(sat) of 1.6V reduces conduction losses
TO-220F isolated package for easy mounting

faeReview.author: James Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Motor Drives
faeReview.content: SL20T65F is a solid choice for 1-3kW motor drive applications. The field-stop technology provides a good balance of conduction and switching losses. I've used this part in numerous VFD designs for pumps and fans, where the 1.6V VCE(sat) helps achieve >95% inverter efficiency. The integrated diode is a nice feature - it saves board space and ensures matched thermal characteristics. The 10μs short-circuit withstand time provides adequate protection margin with modern gate drivers. For best performance, I recommend using a 15V gate drive with negative turn-off (-5V to -8V) to minimize switching losses and improve noise immunity.
faeReview.highlight: Field-stop technology with integrated diode for motor drives

alternativeParts.comparison: SL20T65F=><IKW20N60T: Output current 20A = 20A (same), Package TO-220F = TO-220F (same), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Higher voltage rating with similar performance

companionParts.description: Isolated gate driver for IGBT control

faqs.question: What is the recommended gate drive voltage for SL20T65F?
faqs.answer: SL20T65F should be driven with +15V for turn-on to achieve the specified low VCE(sat). For turn-off, I strongly recommend using negative gate voltage of -5V to -8V. This provides several benefits: faster turn-off reducing switching losses, improved noise immunity preventing false turn-on from dv/dt, better short-circuit protection response, and more robust operation in noisy industrial environments. A typical gate drive scheme uses +15V/-8V. If unipolar drive must be used (0V/+15V), ensure the gate-emitter path has low impedance and consider adding a Miller clamp circuit to prevent dv/dt induced turn-on.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use a gate driver IC with built-in negative voltage capability or a bootstrap circuit with negative bias. Contact our FAE team for gate driver recommendations and circuit design.

faqs.question: What is the maximum power rating for motor drives using SL20T65F?
faqs.answer: SL20T65F can be used in motor drives up to approximately 3-5kW depending on switching frequency and thermal design. At 6kHz switching frequency with natural convection cooling, practical power is around 2-3kW. With forced air cooling or better heatsinking, 4-5kW is achievable. The limiting factors are conduction losses (IC × VCE(sat)) and switching losses (Esw × fsw). For higher power, consider using two devices in parallel or selecting a higher current rated part like SL40T65F. Always verify thermal performance at maximum load and worst-case ambient temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: For designs above 3kW, evaluate thermal performance early. Our FAE team can provide power loss calculations and thermal simulations for your specific motor and operating conditions.

faqs.question: Can SL20T65F be used in bridge configurations?
faqs.answer: Yes, SL20T65F can be used in half-bridge and full-bridge configurations for motor drives and inverters. The 650V rating provides adequate margin for 400V DC bus applications. For bridge topologies, ensure proper dead time control to prevent shoot-through - I recommend minimum 2μs dead time. The integrated anti-parallel diode can conduct reverse current during dead time, which is convenient for motor drive applications. For high-frequency bridge applications (>10kHz), external fast recovery diodes may be needed to reduce switching losses from body diode reverse recovery.
faqs.decisionGuide: For bridge topologies, use a dedicated half-bridge or full-bridge gate driver with integrated dead time control. Contact our FAE team for bridge driver recommendations and layout guidance.

faqs.question: What is short-circuit withstand time and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Short-circuit withstand time (SCWT) is the maximum time an IGBT can sustain a short-circuit condition before failure. SL20T65F has a 10μs SCWT, meaning it can withstand a short circuit for 10 microseconds while the protection circuit detects the fault and shuts down the gate drive. This is critical for motor drives where motor winding shorts or bridge shoot-through can occur. The protection system must detect the overcurrent and shut down within the SCWT. Modern gate drivers with desaturation detection can typically respond in 2-5μs, providing adequate margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always ensure your protection circuit response time is less than the IGBT's SCWT with adequate margin. Contact our FAE team for protection circuit design recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I calculate heatsink requirements for SL20T65F?
faqs.answer: To calculate heatsink requirements, first determine total power dissipation: Ptotal = Pcond + Psw. Conduction loss Pcond = VCE(sat) × IC × D. Switching loss Psw = (Eon + Eoff) × fsw. Then calculate required thermal resistance: RthSA = (Tj_max - Ta) / Ptotal - RthJC - RthCS. For SL20T65F with Tj_max=150°C, Ta=40°C, RthJC=0.8°C/W, RthCS=0.2°C/W, and Ptotal=50W: RthSA = (150-40)/50 - 0.8 - 0.2 = 1.2°C/W. Select a heatsink with RthSA < 1.2°C/W at your airflow condition. Always include 20-30% thermal margin for reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our thermal calculator or contact our FAE team for detailed thermal analysis and heatsink selection based on your specific operating conditions.

products.name: 650V 40A IGBT

With 40A continuous collector current and low saturation voltage of 1.55V typical, this IGBT delivers excellent efficiency in high-power hard-switching applications.
The device includes a fast-recovery anti-parallel diode for freewheeling operation, making it ideal for bridge configurations in industrial motor drives.
features: Field-stop technology for low conduction and switching losses
Low VCE(sat) of 1.55V reduces conduction losses
TO-220F isolated package for easy mounting

faeReview.author: James Wang

faeReview.content: SL40T65F is an excellent choice for 5-10kW motor drive applications. The 40A rating with low VCE(sat) provides excellent efficiency for industrial drives. I've used this part in servo drive designs where the low saturation voltage helps achieve >96% inverter efficiency. The integrated diode saves board space and ensures matched thermal characteristics. For best performance, use a robust gate driver with negative turn-off voltage and adequate dead time control.
faeReview.highlight: High-current IGBT with excellent efficiency for industrial drives

categories.name: SiC Devices
categories.description: Slkor's Silicon Carbide (SiC) power devices including MOSFETs and Schottky diodes for high-frequency, high-efficiency power conversion applications.
categories.longDescription: Slkor's Silicon Carbide (SiC) power devices, available through LiTong distributor, represent the next generation of power semiconductor technology with comprehensive selection guide support. SiC MOSFETs and Schottky diodes offer breakthrough performance compared to silicon devices, enabling higher efficiency, higher frequency operation, and reduced system size. These devices are ideal for EV chargers, solar inverters, data center power supplies, and other applications where efficiency and power density are critical.

series.name: SLC MOSFET Series
series.description: SiC MOSFETs for high-frequency power conversion

series.name: SLC Diode Series
series.description: SiC Schottky diodes for rectification applications

selectionGuide.title: SiC Device Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right SiC device for your high-efficiency application

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right Slkor SiC device?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting Slkor SiC MOSFETs and diodes

faqs.question: What are the main advantages of SiC devices over silicon?
faqs.answer: SiC devices offer several key advantages: Higher breakdown voltage with lower on-resistance, enabling reduced conduction losses; 3-5x faster switching speeds allowing higher frequency operation and smaller passive components; Better thermal conductivity enabling higher operating temperatures (175°C vs 150°C); Lower switching losses reducing EMI and improving efficiency by 2-5%; and Zero reverse recovery charge in SiC diodes eliminating switching losses. These benefits make SiC ideal for applications requiring highest efficiency and power density.
faqs.decisionGuide: For applications where efficiency and power density are critical, SiC devices provide significant benefits. Contact our FAE team for cost-benefit analysis and application guidance.

faqs.question: When should I use SiC MOSFETs vs SiC diodes?
faqs.answer: SiC MOSFETs are used for active switching applications where high-frequency operation and low switching losses are required. They're ideal for primary switches in DC-DC converters, inverters, and PFC circuits. SiC Schottky diodes are used for rectification applications where zero reverse recovery provides significant benefits. They're commonly used as boost diodes in PFC circuits, output rectifiers in LLC converters, and freewheeling diodes in inverters. Many high-efficiency designs use both - SiC MOSFETs for switching and SiC diodes for rectification to maximize efficiency gains.
faqs.decisionGuide: For maximum efficiency improvement, use both SiC MOSFETs and diodes in your design. Contact our FAE team for topology-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What gate drive requirements do SiC MOSFETs have?
faqs.answer: SiC MOSFETs require different gate drive than silicon MOSFETs or IGBTs. Recommended gate drive voltage is +15V to +20V for turn-on (check datasheet for specific device) and -3V to -5V for turn-off. The threshold voltage is lower than silicon devices (typically 2-3V), making them more susceptible to false turn-on from dv/dt. A negative turn-off voltage is strongly recommended to ensure reliable turn-off and prevent shoot-through. Gate drivers should provide fast rise/fall times (<50ns) to take advantage of SiC's fast switching capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use dedicated SiC gate drivers designed for the specific voltage requirements. Contact our FAE team for gate driver recommendations and circuit design.

faqs.question: How do I handle EMI with high-speed SiC switching?
faqs.answer: SiC devices' fast switching (dv/dt up to 50-100V/ns) can create EMI challenges. Mitigation techniques include: Using gate resistors (5-20Ω) to control switching speed; Minimizing loop inductance in power and gate circuits; Implementing proper snubber circuits; Using shielded inductors and transformers; Careful PCB layout with ground planes; and EMI filtering on input and output. The key is to find the right balance - slowing switching too much negates SiC's efficiency benefits, while too fast creates EMI issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with moderate gate resistance and optimize based on EMI measurements. Our FAE team can provide layout guidelines and EMI mitigation strategies.

faqs.question: What is the cost comparison between SiC and silicon devices?
faqs.answer: SiC devices typically cost 3-5x more than equivalent silicon devices. However, system-level cost analysis often shows SiC is cost-effective due to: Reduced cooling requirements (smaller heatsinks or no forced air); Smaller magnetic components due to higher frequency operation; Reduced system size and weight; and Lower energy costs over system lifetime from higher efficiency. For example, a SiC-based EV charger may have 40% higher semiconductor cost but 20% lower system cost due to reduced magnetics and cooling. The payback period depends on application and electricity costs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Perform system-level cost analysis including magnetics, cooling, and energy savings. Contact our FAE team for system cost modeling assistance.

products.name: 650V SiC Schottky Diode
products.shortDescription: 650V 6A SiC Schottky diode with zero reverse recovery for high-efficiency rectification
descriptionParagraphs: SLC06S065 is a 650V silicon carbide Schottky diode designed for high-efficiency power conversion applications.
With zero reverse recovery charge and fast switching characteristics, this diode eliminates switching losses associated with silicon diodes.
The device is ideal for boost PFC circuits, output rectifiers, and freewheeling applications where efficiency is critical.
features: Zero reverse recovery charge eliminates switching losses
High switching speed enables high-frequency operation

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Systems
faeReview.content: SLC06S065 is an excellent choice for PFC boost applications where reverse recovery losses significantly impact efficiency. In a typical 1kW PFC circuit, replacing a silicon diode with this SiC diode can improve efficiency by 0.5-1%. The zero reverse recovery means no switching losses in the diode, and the fast switching doesn't create EMI issues like fast silicon diodes. I've used this part in solar inverters and EV chargers where every fraction of a percent efficiency matters. The 175°C rating provides good thermal margin. For best performance, ensure adequate heatsinking as SiC diodes have positive temperature coefficient.
faeReview.highlight: Zero reverse recovery for maximum efficiency in PFC applications

alternativeParts.comparison: SLC06S065=><C3D06065A: Output current 6A = 6A (same), Package TO-220AC = TO-220AC (same), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Lower forward voltage with equivalent performance

faqs.question: Why is zero reverse recovery important in PFC circuits?
faqs.answer: In PFC boost circuits, the diode reverse recovery causes significant switching losses. When the MOSFET turns on, it must conduct the reverse recovery current of the diode before the diode blocks voltage. This causes: High turn-on losses in the MOSFET; EMI from the sharp current spike; and Efficiency reduction proportional to switching frequency. At 100kHz, reverse recovery losses can be 1-2% of total power. SiC Schottky diodes have zero reverse recovery, eliminating these losses completely. This is why SiC diodes provide the biggest efficiency improvement in hard-switching topologies like PFC boost.
faqs.decisionGuide: For PFC applications above 50kHz, SiC diodes are highly recommended. Contact our FAE team for efficiency calculations and cost-benefit analysis.

faqs.question: Can SiC diodes be paralleled for higher current?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiC Schottky diodes can be paralleled for higher current applications. Unlike silicon diodes, SiC diodes have positive temperature coefficient of forward voltage, meaning they naturally share current when paralleled. As one diode heats up, its forward voltage increases, reducing its current share and balancing with cooler devices. This self-balancing makes paralleling straightforward without external balancing components. However, ensure symmetrical layout with equal trace lengths and impedances to each diode for best current sharing.
faqs.decisionGuide: For currents above 6A, parallel multiple SLC06S065 diodes. Our FAE team can provide layout guidelines for optimal current sharing.

faqs.question: What is the recommended heatsinking for SLC06S065?
faqs.answer: The required heatsinking depends on forward current and duty cycle. At 6A continuous with 1.4V forward voltage, conduction loss is 8.4W. With RthJC=1.5°C/W and RthCS=0.5°C/W, and maximum Tj=175°C: RthSA = (175 - Ta) / 8.4 - 1.5 - 0.5. At 40°C ambient, RthSA = (175-40)/8.4 - 2 = 14°C/W. This allows natural convection cooling with a small heatsink. For higher currents or temperatures, larger heatsinks or forced air may be needed. Always calculate based on your actual operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate thermal requirements based on your specific current and duty cycle. Contact our FAE team for thermal modeling and heatsink selection.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect SiC diode performance?
faqs.answer: SiC diodes have positive temperature coefficient of forward voltage - as temperature increases, forward voltage increases (opposite to silicon diodes). This is beneficial for paralleling as it promotes current sharing. However, it means conduction losses increase at high temperatures. Forward voltage typically increases 20-30% from 25°C to 175°C. Leakage current increases with temperature but remains much lower than silicon diodes even at 175°C. The maximum junction temperature of 175°C provides significant thermal margin compared to silicon's 150°C limit.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for worst-case temperature with adequate margin. Use the forward voltage at operating temperature for loss calculations. Our FAE team can provide temperature-dependent models.

faqs.question: Can SiC diodes replace silicon ultrafast diodes?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiC Schottky diodes can replace silicon ultrafast diodes in most applications with significant benefits. The replacement is straightforward - same package, similar forward voltage, but zero reverse recovery. Benefits include: Elimination of reverse recovery losses; No snubber circuits needed; Higher frequency operation possible; and Cooler operation due to lower losses. The main consideration is cost - SiC diodes are more expensive. The payback comes from improved efficiency, reduced cooling, and potentially smaller magnetics from higher frequency operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high-frequency or high-efficiency applications, SiC diodes are excellent replacements. Contact our FAE team for replacement recommendations and efficiency calculations.

The zero reverse recovery continues to provide significant efficiency improvements over silicon diodes. I've used this part in EV charger designs with excellent thermal performance.

I've used this part in 10kW EV charger designs achieving >98% efficiency. The fast switching requires careful gate drive design and PCB layout, but the efficiency gains are substantial. Use Kelvin source connection for best switching performance.

I've used this part in three-phase EV charger designs with excellent results. The zero reverse recovery continues to provide efficiency benefits even at 1200V.

categories.name: Power Management ICs
categories.description: Slkor's power management ICs including gate drivers, voltage regulators, and protection devices for power system control and monitoring.
categories.longDescription: Slkor's power management IC portfolio, available through LiTong distributor, provides essential control and monitoring functions for power electronic systems with comprehensive selection guide support. The product line includes gate drivers for MOSFET and IGBT control, voltage regulators for power supply generation, and protection ICs for system safety. These devices are designed to work seamlessly with Slkor's power semiconductors, providing complete system solutions.

series.name: SL Gate Driver Series
series.description: Low-side and isolated gate drivers for power switches

series.name: SL LDO Series
series.description: Low-dropout voltage regulators for power supply applications

selectionGuide.title: Power Management IC Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right power management IC for your application

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right Slkor power management IC?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting Slkor gate drivers, regulators, and protection ICs

faqs.question: What types of gate drivers does Slkor offer?
faqs.answer: Slkor offers several types of gate drivers: Low-side drivers for single ground-referenced switches with fast propagation delay and high peak current; Isolated drivers with galvanic isolation for high-side and bridge applications; Half-bridge drivers with built-in dead time control and interlock protection; and IGBT drivers with desaturation detection and soft shutdown. All drivers feature under-voltage lockout (UVLO) to ensure proper switch operation and Miller clamp to prevent false turn-on.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select driver type based on your topology - low-side for simple switches, isolated for high-side or bridges. Contact our FAE team for driver selection and circuit design.

faqs.question: How do I select the right gate driver for my MOSFET?
faqs.answer: Gate driver selection depends on several factors: Peak current capability should be sufficient to charge/discharge the MOSFET gate quickly - calculate as Ipeak = Qg / tr (where tr is desired rise time); Propagation delay affects timing accuracy in bridge circuits; UVLO threshold should match your supply voltage; and Isolation voltage rating for high-voltage applications. For a MOSFET with 20nC gate charge and desired 50ns rise time, you need Ipeak = 20nC/50ns = 400mA minimum. Select a driver with at least 2A peak current for margin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required peak current based on gate charge and desired switching speed. Our FAE team can recommend optimal drivers for your specific MOSFETs.

faqs.question: What is Miller clamp and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Miller clamp is a feature in gate drivers that prevents false turn-on of MOSFETs during high dv/dt switching. When the switch turns on, the dv/dt across the drain-source couples through the Miller capacitance (Cgd) to the gate, potentially raising gate voltage above threshold. The Miller clamp provides a low-impedance path to ground when gate voltage drops below a threshold (typically 2V), preventing the Miller current from charging the gate. This is especially important in bridge circuits where shoot-through could occur if both switches turn on simultaneously.
faqs.decisionGuide: For bridge topologies and high dv/dt applications, use gate drivers with Miller clamp feature. Contact our FAE team for Miller clamp operation details.

faqs.question: What protection features should I look for in gate drivers?
faqs.answer: Important gate driver protection features include: Under-voltage lockout (UVLO) - disables output when supply voltage is too low to properly turn on the switch; Over-current/desaturation detection - monitors switch voltage to detect short circuits and initiates soft shutdown; Thermal shutdown - protects the driver IC from overheating; and Interlock/dead time - prevents both high-side and low-side switches from turning on simultaneously in bridge configurations. For IGBT applications, desaturation protection is essential to protect against short circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: For robust designs, select drivers with comprehensive protection features. Our FAE team can recommend drivers with appropriate protection for your application.

faqs.question: How do I power the high-side gate driver in a half-bridge circuit?
faqs.answer: High-side gate drivers in half-bridge circuits typically use bootstrap power supply. A bootstrap capacitor (typically 0.1-1μF) is charged from the low-voltage supply through a diode when the low-side switch is on. When the low-side turns off and high-side turns on, the bootstrap capacitor provides the floating supply for the high-side driver. The bootstrap capacitor must be sized to maintain voltage above UVLO threshold during the high-side on-time. For 100% duty cycle or very long on-times, an isolated DC-DC supply may be needed instead of bootstrap.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use bootstrap for most applications with <95% duty cycle. For 100% duty cycle, use isolated power supply. Contact our FAE team for bootstrap capacitor sizing calculations.

products.name: Single-Channel Low-Side Gate Driver
products.shortDescription: High-speed low-side gate driver with 4A peak current and fast propagation delay for MOSFET and IGBT control
descriptionParagraphs: SL27517 is a high-speed single-channel low-side gate driver designed to drive MOSFETs and IGBTs in power switching applications.
With 4A peak source/sink current and 25ns typical propagation delay, this driver enables fast switching for high-frequency applications.
The device features under-voltage lockout (UVLO) to ensure proper switch operation and input hysteresis for noise immunity.
features: 4A peak source/sink current for fast switching

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Electronics
faeReview.content: SL27517 is a versatile gate driver that works well with a wide range of MOSFETs and IGBTs. The 4A drive capability is sufficient for devices up to about 50nC gate charge with good switching speed. I've used this driver in DC-DC converters up to 500kHz with excellent results. The wide supply voltage range (4.5-20V) provides flexibility in system design. The propagation delay is consistent across temperature, which is important for timing-critical applications. For best performance, place the driver close to the power switch with minimal gate loop inductance. I recommend using a 10-22Ω gate resistor to control switching speed and reduce EMI.
faeReview.highlight: 4A drive capability with fast 25ns propagation delay

alternativeParts.comparison: SL27517=><UCC27517: Output current 4A = 4A (same), Package SOT-23-5 = SOT-23-5 (same), suitable for direct replacement

faqs.question: What size gate resistor should I use with SL27517?
faqs.answer: Gate resistor selection balances switching speed and EMI. For SL27517 with 4A drive capability, typical gate resistors range from 0Ω to 47Ω. Start with 10Ω and adjust based on switching waveforms and EMI measurements. Lower values (0-10Ω) provide fastest switching but higher EMI; higher values (22-47Ω) reduce EMI but increase switching losses. For high-frequency applications (>200kHz), use lower values to minimize switching losses. For EMI-sensitive applications, use higher values or add ferrite beads. Always verify switching waveforms with an oscilloscope to ensure clean transitions without excessive ringing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 10Ω and optimize based on switching waveforms and EMI testing. Our FAE team can provide gate resistor selection guidelines for your specific application.

faqs.question: Can SL27517 drive IGBTs as well as MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Yes, SL27517 can drive both MOSFETs and IGBTs. For MOSFETs, the +12V to +15V output provides optimal RDS(on). For IGBTs, +15V is recommended for turn-on to achieve low VCE(sat). The 4A peak current is sufficient for most IGBTs up to about 100A rating. However, for IGBTs, negative turn-off voltage (-5V to -8V) is often recommended, which SL27517 cannot provide directly. For IGBT applications requiring negative bias, use an isolated driver or add a negative bias circuit. The driver works well for IGBT applications where unipolar drive (0V/+15V) is acceptable.
faqs.decisionGuide: For MOSFETs and small IGBTs, SL27517 works well. For large IGBTs requiring negative bias, consider isolated drivers. Contact our FAE team for IGBT drive circuit recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the maximum switching frequency for SL27517?
faqs.answer: SL27517 can operate at switching frequencies up to several MHz, but practical limits depend on the power switch and application. With 25ns propagation delay and fast rise/fall times, the driver itself doesn't limit frequency. The limiting factors are: Gate drive power (P = Qg × Vgs × fsw) - at 1MHz with 20nC gate charge and 12V drive, power is 240mW; Switching losses in the power device; and EMI at high frequencies. For most power applications, 100kHz to 1MHz is the practical range. The driver is well-suited for high-frequency DC-DC converters and Class-D audio amplifiers.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high-frequency applications, verify gate drive power dissipation and thermal performance. Our FAE team can help optimize switching frequency for your design.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in the gate driver?
faqs.answer: Gate driver power dissipation comes from two sources: Quiescent power (Pq = Vcc × Iq) - typically very low (2μA standby for SL27517); and Gate drive power (Pd = Qg × Vgs × fsw). For example, driving a MOSFET with 20nC gate charge at 12V and 100kHz: Pd = 20nC × 12V × 100kHz = 24mW. This is dissipated in the driver's output stage. Total power is typically 20-100mW depending on switching frequency and gate charge. Ensure the package can handle this power at maximum ambient temperature. SOT-23-5 has thermal resistance of about 200°C/W, so 100mW would cause 20°C temperature rise.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power dissipation based on your gate charge and switching frequency. Contact our FAE team for thermal analysis and package selection.

faqs.question: What layout considerations are important for gate drivers?
faqs.answer: Proper PCB layout is critical for gate driver performance: Minimize gate loop inductance by placing driver close to power switch with short, wide traces; Use Kelvin connection for source/emitter return to avoid common source inductance; Place bypass capacitor (0.1-1μF ceramic) close to driver supply pins; Keep high dv/dt traces (gate drive) away from sensitive signals; Use ground plane for return currents; and For high-current drivers, consider thermal vias under the package. Poor layout can cause ringing, EMI, and even device failure from voltage overshoot.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow layout best practices for optimal performance. Our FAE team can review your PCB layout and provide recommendations.

products.name: Dual-Channel Low-Side Gate Driver

features: Dual independent channels with 4A drive capability
Independent enable inputs for each channel

faeReview.author: Michael Chen

faeReview.content: SL27524 is an excellent choice for synchronous buck converter designs. The dual-channel configuration with independent enables provides flexible control for high-side and low-side switches. The matched propagation delay ensures minimal dead time, improving efficiency. I've used this part in multi-phase VRM designs with excellent results. The 4A drive capability handles large MOSFETs with ease.
faeReview.highlight: Dual-channel driver ideal for synchronous buck converters

products.name: 300mA Low-Noise LDO Regulator
products.shortDescription: Low-noise 300mA LDO with high PSRR and low quiescent current for sensitive analog and RF circuits
descriptionParagraphs: SL6206 is a low-noise low-dropout (LDO) voltage regulator designed for powering sensitive analog and RF circuits.
With 300mA output current, high PSRR, and low output noise, this LDO provides clean power for precision applications.
The low quiescent current of 50μA makes it suitable for battery-powered applications.

faeReview.author: Sarah Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Analog Power
faeReview.content: SL6206 provides excellent noise performance for sensitive analog circuits. The 70dB PSRR effectively rejects switching noise from upstream DC-DC converters. I've used this part in RF transceiver designs where clean power is critical. The 300mA current capability is sufficient for most analog subsystems. For best noise performance, add a small ceramic capacitor (0.01μF) on the bypass pin.
faeReview.highlight: Low-noise LDO ideal for RF and analog circuits

products.name: Half-Bridge Gate Driver with Bootstrap
products.shortDescription: High-voltage half-bridge gate driver with integrated bootstrap diode and programmable dead time for motor drives and power converters
descriptionParagraphs: SL4427 is a high-voltage half-bridge gate driver designed for motor drive and power converter applications.
With integrated bootstrap diode and programmable dead time, this driver simplifies half-bridge and full-bridge designs.
The 600V high-side capability and 4A peak drive current handle a wide range of power switches.
features: Integrated bootstrap diode simplifies power supply
4A peak drive current for fast switching

faeReview.author: James Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Motor Drives
faeReview.content: SL4427 is a versatile half-bridge driver for motor drive applications. The integrated bootstrap diode saves board space and cost. The programmable dead time is essential for preventing shoot-through in bridge circuits. I've used this part in BLDC motor drive designs with excellent reliability. The 600V rating handles most industrial motor drive applications.
faeReview.highlight: Half-bridge driver with integrated bootstrap for motor drives
